props.Notable Incidents: Fall menu launch night

blocks.text: Service Notes
Fall menu launch — new pumpkin gnocchi and braised short rib specials debuted. Gnocchi moved 35 plates, short rib moved 28. Both are keepers. Tony outdid himself on the presentation.
Bar did well — Maria created a fall cocktail flight (apple cider Negroni, pear Bellini, bourbon-fig old fashioned). Sold 40 flights at $28 each. Wine-by-the-glass revenue up 12% over last Friday.
Follow-up
Confirm Sysco can increase short rib order for next week
Get Tony's updated pricing for fall specials to Giancarlo for Thursday call

props.Notable Incidents: Health inspection — PASSED

blocks.text: Health Inspection

James Okonkwo from the Milwaukee Health Department came by at 11:30 AM, just before lunch service. Full walkthrough: kitchen, walk-in, dry storage, bar, restrooms. Passed with 96/100. Only dings: one handwashing sign partially obscured behind new shelving (fixed on the spot) and a sanitizer bucket concentration slightly low at one station.
James said we're one of the best-maintained restaurants on his route. Feels good. Next quarterly inspection estimated late March / early April 2026.
Service Notes
Quiet Wednesday otherwise. 124 covers. 86'd branzino by 8 PM — delivery was short this morning.

props.Notable Incidents: Solid Saturday.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Great Saturday. Fall menu continuing to perform well. Pumpkin gnocchi is the clear hit — had to 86 it twice during service because we underprepped. Tony adjusting par levels.
Walk-in at 37°F all day. Temperatures logged and consistent.
Carmen closed. Register balanced within $12. Clean night.

props.Notable Incidents: Holiday party booking inquiries picking up.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Busy Saturday. Holiday party season is beginning — took 4 inquiry calls this week for corporate holiday dinners in December. Two already confirmed with deposits incoming. Pipeline looking strong.
Daniela managed a tricky 16-top walk-in group beautifully — rearranged the dining room in 10 minutes. She's invaluable in FOH.
All registers balanced. Good close.

props.Notable Incidents: Sysco price increase notice. 200+ cover Saturday.

blocks.text: Service Notes
First 200+ cover Saturday of the fall season. Kitchen ran tight but clean. Tony had everyone sharp. Bar crushed it with the fall cocktail menu.
Vendor Issue

Dan Mallory from Sysco called — they're raising prices 3-5% across proteins effective December 1. Beef and seafood hit hardest. Need to review our food cost percentages and decide whether to absorb or adjust menu prices. Will discuss with Giancarlo on Thursday.
Explored US Foods pricing with Keisha Brown — they're competitive on some items. May shift backup orders there for proteins.

props.Staff Issues: Jessica Hartmann requested schedule change — avoiding shifts with bartender Ryan Engel.
props.Notable Incidents: Corporate holiday party for 30 guests (private dining).

blocks.text: Service Notes
225 covers with a 30-person corporate holiday party in the private dining room. Kitchen was stretched but managed. The party alone generated $4,800 in food and bar revenue.

Jessica pulled me aside — asked to not be scheduled on the same shifts as Ryan Engel (bartender). She was visibly upset. Didn't share details and I didn't push. Adjusted the schedule starting next week. No documentation needed unless it escalates.

props.Notable Incidents: Pre-Thanksgiving rush. Highest cover count of the month.

blocks.text: Service Notes

Pre-Thanksgiving Saturday — packed from 6 PM to close. 235 covers, our highest this month. Bar revenue alone was $5,200. Maria worked a double to cover for Ryan who called out sick (convenient timing — Jessica was on tonight, noted).
Thanksgiving menu preview special moved well. Tony did a butternut squash ravioli with brown butter sage that got audible reactions from tables.
Carmen closed. Register balanced perfectly. I'm heading home to deal with Alejandro's visit tomorrow. He's in town for Thanksgiving.

props.Staff Issues: Luis Ochoa late 15 min — bus issues. No write-up.
props.Notable Incidents: Holiday party season in full swing.

blocks.text: Service Notes

Holiday parties are hitting their stride. Three private events this week alone. Tonight had a 20-top holiday dinner plus full regular service. Kitchen ran clean. Tony is a machine during party season.
Champagne consumption is way up. Robert Fontaine at Capital Wine is sending an extra case of Prosecco midweek. Revenue per cover is up 15% vs October — holiday spending.
Walk-in at 38°F. Acceptable but I want it lower for inspection prep. Adjusting thermostat.

props.Notable Incidents: Highest cover count of 2025. Back-to-back holiday events.

blocks.text: Service Notes

Monster Saturday. 245 covers — highest of the year. Two simultaneous holiday parties (25-top and 18-top in private dining sections) plus a packed main floor. Kitchen went through every protein we had. 86'd truffle risotto by 8:30 and Dover sole by 9.
Bar revenue: $6,400. Maria ran out of Prosecco at 9:45 PM. Note: double the Prosecco order for the Dec 19-31 stretch.
Staff was incredible tonight. Tony, Carmen, Daniela, Maria — everyone was sharp. These are the nights I'm most proud to run this place.

props.Notable Incidents: Register discrepancy found from Tuesday night's close.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Solid Friday. Holiday season continues strong. No 86'd items — Sysco delivery was complete for once.
⚠️ Register Issue
Tuesday night's register was short $180. Carmen was closing. First discrepancy I've noticed — probably nothing, but making a note.
I didn't say anything to Carmen. Probably just a miscounted till. Noting it here in case it happens again.

props.Notable Incidents: Christmas Eve service.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Christmas Eve. Ran a special prix fixe menu — 4 courses at $85pp. Well received. Lots of families. 168 covers, most booked weeks in advance. Kitchen closed at 9:30 so staff could get home.
I closed the register myself tonight. Balanced to the penny. Smooth evening. Heading home to be with Mom.
Merry Christmas to Bella Notte. It's been a good year.

props.Notable Incidents: New Year's Eve — record revenue night.

blocks.text: Service Notes
NYE prix fixe: 5 courses at $135pp plus champagne toast. 210 covers, fully booked by Dec 15. Revenue: $24,600 — record for a single night. Bar added another $7,200 on top.
Kitchen was flawless. Tony ran the most organized NYE service I've seen in 8 years at Bella Notte. Timing on the midnight champagne toast was perfect.
I poured champagne for over 200 people tonight. Handled it. Focused on the logistics and the smiles. Called Dave on the drive home. He said he's proud of me. Good way to end the year.
Carmen closed tonight. Will reconcile tomorrow. Here's hoping it's clean.

props.Notable Incidents: Post-holiday slowdown begins.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Classic January slump. 118 covers on a Saturday — down 50% from December peaks. Expected. Everyone is broke and dieting after the holidays. Lunch was dead (22 covers).
Used the downtime to deep clean the kitchen and start health inspection prep. Tony reorganized the walk-in. Good use of slow days.
NYE register (12/31) reconciled clean. Carmen's close. Balanced. Maybe the December thing was a one-off.

props.Notable Incidents: Register shortage — $180 from Jan 8 close. Immigration audit notice arrived.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue
Jan 8 close: Carmen's register is short $180. First confirmed discrepancy. $180 short. Could still be coincidence but I'm uncomfortable. Starting a formal log in Airtable.
Immigration Audit

Received a letter from the Wisconsin Department of Workforce Development — routine I-9 audit. They want employment verification records for all current staff within 30 days. This is going to be a problem for Luis. His SSN may not validate. I need to talk to Sandra about this.
If Luis gets flagged, I lose a line cook who's critical for the kitchen AND whose referral connected me to Rosa (Mom's aide). This could cascade.
Service Notes
Quiet Friday otherwise. January business is flat. Using the downtime to prepare for the audit and health inspection.

props.Notable Incidents: Second register discrepancy — pattern forming.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue — Escalating
Reconciling Sunday's close (Jan 18 — Carmen's shift). Register short $340. Second discrepancy. Running total: $520 across 2 incidents.

Started pulling old register reports. Going back through every close since November. Every discrepancy is on Carmen's shifts. Daniela's closes: perfect. My closes: perfect. Carmen: two shorts in ten days.

I don't want to believe this. Carmen is reliable, the staff trusts her, and she watches Mom. But the data doesn't lie. Logging everything in Airtable. Not confronting yet — need to see if the pattern holds.

props.Notable Incidents: Comfort Keepers billing dispute. Health inspection timeline confirmed.

blocks.text: Comfort Keepers

Reviewed Mom's aide invoices going back to October. Comfort Keepers has been billing $2,400/month — contract rate is $1,800/month. That's $600/month overcharge for 4 months = $2,400 so far. Called their billing department. Got the runaround. Sandra says to file a formal dispute in writing.
Health Inspection
Called James Okonkwo's office. Next inspection window is mid-to-late March. Started our 2-month prep checklist. Tony taking the lead on kitchen readiness.
Service Notes
Quiet Thursday. January continues to be slow. Gives me time to deal with all this administrative mess.

props.Notable Incidents: Third register shortage. Three in one month.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue
Jan 30 close (Friday, Carmen): register short $280. Third. Running total: $800 across 3 incidents.

Cross-referenced every close since October 1 in Airtable. Carmen closed 22 times in this period. Three of those had shortages. The other 19 balanced within normal tolerance. No other closer has had a single discrepancy.
I need to talk to Sandra about my legal obligations here. As GM, if I know about potential theft and don't report it to Giancarlo, what's my liability?
Service Notes
Saturday was decent — business picking up slightly as we head into February. 148 covers. Walk-in holding at 37°F.

props.Notable Incidents: Fourth register shortage — $420, biggest yet.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue — Biggest Yet
Feb 7 close (Saturday, Carmen): register short $420. Biggest discrepancy yet. Fourth. Running total: $1,220 across 4 incidents.
Saturday was a 180-cover night. Higher cash volume. Maybe she's taking more when there's more cash in the drawer? That suggests calculation, not carelessness.
I feel sick writing this. Carmen has been with us for three years. She was the first person I called when Mom needed someone to watch her in the evenings. But $1,220 doesn't just vanish.
Spoke with Sandra briefly — she confirmed I have an obligation to document and will eventually need to inform Giancarlo. But she wants me to have an airtight record first.

props.Staff Issues: All hands on deck. No call-outs.
props.Notable Incidents: Valentine's Day — record cover count.

blocks.text: Service Notes

Valentine's Day. 262 covers — record for a non-NYE night. Special prix fixe at $95pp with champagne toast. Kitchen was at maximum capacity from 5:30 PM to close. Tony ran 6 courses simultaneously for the prix fixe tables while maintaining the regular menu for walk-ins.
Bar revenue: $7,800. Maria's Valentine's cocktail (Amore Amaro — Aperol, blood orange, prosecco) was the #1 seller. She sold 90 of them.
Every single staff member showed up tonight. No call-outs. That matters more than anything.
I closed the register myself tonight. $26,200 in food revenue alone. This night funds half of January's shortfall. Balanced to the penny.

props.Notable Incidents: First Lenten fish fry for St. Boniface. Fifth register shortage from Feb 19.

blocks.text: Catering: St. Boniface Fish Fry Week 1

First fish fry pickup went smoothly. 65 portions beer-battered cod, coleslaw, fries, tartar sauce, dinner rolls. Church volunteers picked up at 4:30 PM. Father Nowak texted later — parish loved it. Good start to the series.
⚠️ Register Issue
Feb 19 close (Thursday, Carmen): short $150. Smallest amount yet. Fifth. Running total: $1,370 across 5. I've logged it in Airtable.
The smaller amount is interesting. Thursday was a lighter night — maybe she takes less when there's less cash? More evidence of deliberate calculation.
Service Notes
Busy Friday otherwise. Lenten fish demand is also boosting dine-in orders — added a beer-battered cod to the regular menu and it moved 45 plates tonight.

props.Notable Incidents: Fish fry series going well. Strong Saturday.

blocks.text: Service Notes

Good Saturday. Fish fry series is bringing in some new faces — people who come for the fish fry on Friday and then come back Saturday for the full experience. Tony's calling it the 'fish fry funnel.' Smart.
Walk-in at 37°F. All temperature logs up to date in Airtable. Inspection prep on track.
Carmen is closing tonight. I'll reconcile tomorrow.

props.Notable Incidents: Sixth register shortage from Feb 28 — $310.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue
Feb 28 close (Saturday, Carmen): short $310. Sixth. Running total: $1,680 across 6.

Six incidents. Every single one on Carmen's close. Zero on mine or Daniela's. The pattern is statistically impossible to explain as coincidence. Sandra says I need to start planning the confrontation and figure out the Mom-care contingency.
Service Notes
Quiet Sunday. March is coming — fish fry season, health inspection, quinceañera. Busy month ahead.

props.Notable Incidents: Seventh register shortage — $290. Fish fry week 3 went well.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue
Mar 7 close (Saturday, Carmen): short $290. Seventh. Running total: $1,970 across 7. The amounts range from $150 to $430 but the pattern is clear.
Updated the Airtable log and my private investigation notes in Notion. Sandra wants to meet this week to discuss next steps.
Catering: Fish Fry Week 3
80 portions this week, up from 72 last week. Father Nowak says attendance is growing. Added 10% more fries per his request.
Service Notes
Quiet Sunday. Good day — Mom was lucid and happy. Made tamales with Rosa. Brought some to the restaurant. Tony says they're better than anything on our menu.

props.Notable Incidents: Giancarlo call — Q1 review.

blocks.text: Owner Call

2 PM call with Giancarlo. Reviewed Q1 numbers: revenue trending 4% above last year. Catering pipeline strong — $45K in deposits held. He asked about the health inspection timeline. I told him mid-to-late March. He's happy.
Did NOT discuss: register situation, immigration notice, or personal matters. Keeping things clean until I have a plan.
Service Notes
Good Friday. Full dining room by 7:30. Pushed the specials board hard — moved 30 covers of the truffle risotto.

props.Notable Incidents: Eighth register shortage — $430. Worst yet. Running total $2,400 confirmed.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Register Issue — Pattern Undeniable
Mar 14 close (Saturday, Carmen): short $430. The biggest single discrepancy yet. Eighth. Running total: $2,400 across 8 incidents. All on Carmen's closes.
Carmen closed every single one of these nights. Every other closer — Daniela, me — reconciled perfectly. The pattern is undeniable.
This was also my sobriety anniversary — 3 years yesterday. And I'm sitting here documenting theft by the woman who watches my mother. What a day.

props.Notable Incidents: Walk-in cooler temp incident. Fish fry week 5.

blocks.text: ⚠️ Walk-In Cooler Temperature

Morning check: walk-in was at 42°F. Two degrees above the 40°F safe limit. Tony caught it at opening and we immediately adjusted the thermostat and moved sensitive proteins to the backup reach-in. By 1 PM it was back to 37°F.

Root cause: the compressor cycled off overnight and took too long to restart. Need to have the HVAC tech look at it Monday. Logged in Airtable temperature tracker. Health inspection is coming — this cannot happen again.
Catering: Fish Fry Week 5
80 portions. Smooth pickup at 4:30. Father Nowak says the parish is thrilled — best fish fry they've had in years.
Service Notes
Big Friday. Lenten fish fry catering pickup plus full dinner service. Kitchen stretched but Tony managed the line well. Bar crushed it — Maria ran a Negroni flight that moved $2,200 in revenue.
Carmen closed tonight. Register balanced to within $8. Clean close.

props.Notable Incidents: Alvarez quinceañera at Bella Notte — 100 guests.

blocks.text: Catering: Alvarez Quinceañera

100 guests in the private dining room and outdoor terrace. Menu: antipasto display, chicken marsala, pasta primavera, tiramisu station. Tres leches cake provided by family. Mariachi band arrived at 7 PM through rear entrance. Setup began at 3 PM, service 5:30–10 PM.
Patricia Alvarez was emotional and thrilled. Tipped the catering crew $400 cash. Event came in on budget — deposit of $3,200 covered most of it, final balance of $1,600 invoiced and paid.
Tony ran the catering kitchen while Carmen and Maria managed regular dine-in service. 145 covers on the regular floor despite being down staff for the event. Marco and Luis held the line together.
Proud of this crew tonight. We ran two simultaneous operations and both were excellent.

props.Staff Issues: Jessica called out — flu. Daniela covered her section.
props.Notable Incidents: None. Quiet Wednesday.

blocks.text: Service Notes
Solid midweek service. Lunch was light (38 covers), dinner picked up nicely. New osso buco special moved well — sold 22 plates. Tony's instinct on the braising time was right.
Walk-in at 38°F all day. No issues. HVAC tech came Monday — replaced the compressor relay. Should prevent last week's temperature spike from recurring.
Follow-up
Call Sysco about halibut shortage — second week in a row
Check Jessica's schedule for rest of week
Kitchen Opening Checklist (10:00 AM)
Clock in via Toast POS.
Check walk-in cooler temperature — must read ≤40°F. Log in Airtable temperature tracker. If above 40°F, notify Victor immediately.
Check freezer temperature — must read ≤0°F. Log in Airtable.
Receive and verify morning deliveries (Sysco Tue/Thu/Sat, Milwaukee Bread Co. daily). Compare packing slip to PO.
Review prep list posted by sous chef. Assign stations.
Fire up equipment: ovens to 425°F, flattop, fryers to 350°F.
Set up mise en place for lunch service.
Sanitize all cutting boards and prep surfaces. Replace sanitizer buckets.
Check handwashing stations — soap, paper towels, signage.
Notify FOH when kitchen is ready for service.
Kitchen Closing Checklist (Post-Service)
Break down all stations. Wrap and label all prep items with date and contents.
All proteins returned to walk-in. FIFO — new items behind old.
Clean flattop, grill grates, fryer baskets. Strain and filter fryer oil.
Sanitize all prep surfaces, cutting boards, and equipment.
Sweep and mop all kitchen floors including under equipment.
Take out trash and recycling. Replace liners.
Log final walk-in and freezer temperatures in Airtable.
Verify all gas equipment is off. Check pilot lights on ovens.
Set dishwasher to final sanitize cycle. Verify water temp hits 180°F.
Lock kitchen entrance. Notify closing manager that BOH is secured.
Pre-Inspection Deep Clean (2 Weeks Before)
Deep clean all walk-in shelving — remove items, sanitize, reorganize FIFO
Degrease hood vents and exhaust filters
Verify all food items have date labels
Check and calibrate all thermometers
Inspect pest control traps — replace if needed
Verify handwashing signage (English and Spanish)
Review employee food handler certifications
Test dishwasher sanitize cycle — must hit 180°F
Check first aid kit
Review temperature logs for past 30 days — ensure no gaps
Day-Of Inspection Prep
Walk-in temp ≤38°F (give margin below 40°F limit)
All staff in clean uniforms, hair nets/hats, no jewelry
Ensure Victor or Tony available to walk with inspector
Have previous inspection report and corrective actions ready

props.Venue: Bella Notte Ristorante — Private Dining Room + Outdoor Terrace

blocks.text: Event Details

Quinceañera celebration for Sofia Alvarez. 100 guests. Cocktail hour 5:30–6:30 PM, dinner service 6:30–9:00 PM, dessert and dancing 9:00–10:00 PM. Private dining room plus outdoor terrace with heat lamps.
Menu
Cocktail Hour: Antipasto display (prosciutto, salami, marinated vegetables, fresh mozzarella, focaccia), bruschetta trio, arancini
Entrée (plated, choice of): Chicken Marsala with roasted fingerling potatoes OR Pasta Primavera with seasonal vegetables (vegetarian)
Sides: House salad with balsamic vinaigrette, garlic bread
Dessert: Tiramisu station (individual cups), tres leches cake (provided by family), coffee service
Dietary Notes
3 guests gluten-free (GF pasta available). 1 nut allergy — no pine nuts in any dish. 12 vegetarian entrées confirmed.
Staffing
Tony Aguilar (kitchen lead), Marco Villareal, Luis Ochoa (kitchen). Daniela Perez (FOH coordination), 3 servers. Victor overseeing logistics and client communication.
Budget
Deposit: $3,200. Final balance: $1,600. Total: $4,800. Paid in full. Patricia tipped catering crew $400 cash.

pages.title: St. Boniface Fish Fry Series — Lenten Fridays 2026

props.Venue: Pickup from Bella Notte kitchen → St. Boniface Church Parish Hall

blocks.text: Series Overview
7 consecutive Friday fish fry events during Lent (Feb 20 through Apr 3, 2026). Pickup format — church volunteers handle service at parish hall. Bella Notte provides food only.
Weekly Menu (Fixed)
Beer-battered Atlantic cod (60–100 portions depending on week)
Classic coleslaw
French fries (par-fried, finished at parish hall)
Tartar sauce and lemon wedges
Dinner rolls (from Milwaukee Bread Co.)
Logistics
Pickup at Bella Notte kitchen: Fridays at 4:30 PM. Church volunteer driver picks up in insulated carriers. Food provided in disposable steam trays.
NOTE: St. Boniface is also where I attend Wednesday and Saturday AA meetings. Father Nowak knows me from both contexts. Keep it professional during pickup.
Completed Weeks
Feb 20 — 65 portions. Smooth.
Feb 27 — 72 portions. They asked for more fries next time.
Mar 6 — 80 portions. Added 10% more fries.
Mar 13 — 78 portions. Cod quality excellent from Sysco.
Mar 20 — 80 portions. Smooth.
Mar 27 — 85 portions (requested increase). Pending.
Apr 3 — Final week. TBD on count.

Six months to three years. Started this journal when I hit two years — March 14, 2024. Back then I wasn't sure I'd make it to two and a half. Now the math says I'll hit three years in March and the idea doesn't feel impossible anymore. It feels like Tuesday.

Work is steady. Fall menu launch this week — Tony is excited about the new short ribs. I'm excited about not having to taste the wine pairings Robert keeps pushing. Maria handles all of that. She doesn't know why. Nobody does.
Mom had a good week. Two episodes, but both mild — she called me Eduardo twice and then corrected herself. Rosa says she's eating well. Donepezil seems to be holding steady.

Dave and I talked at Saturday's meeting about what year three means. He said it's the year you stop counting days and start counting what you're building. I like that. I'm building something here — for Mom, for the restaurant, for myself.
Routine entry. Things are calm. Fall menu is performing well. Restaurant is humming. Mom is good — more good days than bad this month. Made it to both meetings this week.

No cravings. Not even a whisper. Some weeks are like this — the disease goes quiet and I almost forget it's there. Dave says those are the dangerous weeks because you start believing you're cured. Nobody is cured. I know that.
Stable. Grateful. Moving forward.

Thanksgiving. Alejandro came. He hasn't been to Milwaukee since Easter. He took one look at Mom — she was having a confused afternoon, kept calling him Victor and calling me Eduardo — and he lost it. 'She needs to be in a facility, Victor. You're in denial. This is elder abuse.'

Elder abuse. My own brother called what I'm doing elder abuse. I'm the one who moved her in. I'm the one who hired Rosa. I'm the one who drives her to Dr. Pham and sits with her at 3 AM when she's scared. He flies in from Phoenix twice a year and tells me I'm failing.

After he left, I drove to the restaurant. The bar was closed — we're dark on Thanksgiving — but I have the keys. I stood in front of the liquor shelf for I don't know how long. Maker's Mark, eye level, like always. I could feel it. The pull. The promise of 20 minutes where none of this matters.

I didn't drink. I called Dave from the parking lot. He talked me down. Reminded me that Alejandro is the disease's favorite recruiting tool — anything that makes me feel powerless. I'm not powerless. I'm just exhausted.
Missed the Saturday meeting because of Thanksgiving. Need to get back tomorrow. Need it bad.

Poured champagne for 60 people tonight. Holiday party in the private dining room — popping corks, golden bubbles, everyone laughing and toasting. I poured every glass with a smile and felt the familiar ache behind my ribs.

Holiday season is the hardest stretch. Every table wants wine pairings. Every corporate party wants cocktail hour. I'm surrounded by it from 11 AM to midnight, six days a week. Maria handles the tastings but I'm still the one carrying the bottles from the bar, still the one signing off on the liquor orders, still the one who locks up alone with a hundred bottles at 1 AM.
Also noticed something off with the register tonight. Carmen's close. Cash drawer felt light. Probably nothing but it nagged at me.
Missed the Wednesday meeting — couldn't leave during the party. Two weeks in a row without a meeting. That's not good. Saturday I go no matter what.

Survived the hardest night of the year. NYE. Champagne toast at midnight for 210 people. I raised a glass of sparkling water and nobody noticed or cared. That's the strange mercy of this job — nobody is watching the GM's glass.

During the countdown I felt it. Not a craving exactly — more like grief. Grief for the version of me who used to love this night. Who used to drink champagne at midnight and feel invincible. That guy nearly died on a kitchen floor. I have to keep remembering that.
Called Dave on the drive home. He said 'You just poured champagne for 210 people and you're calling me sober. That's not surviving — that's thriving.' I don't feel like I'm thriving. But I'm here.
New year. Let's see what 2026 brings.
Discovered the register discrepancies are a pattern. Three incidents since December — all on Carmen's closes. I started a formal log. The amounts aren't huge individually but they're adding up.

Can't tell Dave the details — it's a work thing and I don't want to burden him with specifics about potential employee theft. But I told him I'm stressed about a situation at the restaurant that I can't control. He said to focus on what I can control: my sobriety, my meetings, my next right action.

The immigration audit notice is also weighing on me. Luis is a good kid. If his SSN doesn't validate, I lose a line cook and potentially damage the relationship with Rosa, which affects Mom's care. Everything is connected. Pull one thread and the whole thing unravels.
Made it to Wednesday meeting. Didn't share but being there helped. The cravings are low but the anxiety is high. Different beast, same disease.
Valentine's Day. 262 covers. Busiest non-NYE night in the restaurant's history. The bar was packed — Maria sold 90 specialty cocktails. I was on my feet for 14 hours straight.

The bar was gorgeous tonight. Maria had it lit up with candles, the Negroni flights glowing amber, couples leaning into each other over cocktails. Beautiful. And for a moment I wanted to be on the other side of that bar — not serving, but sitting. Ordering a Barolo and letting the night soften.

I didn't. I closed the register myself — $26,200 in food, another $7,800 at the bar. Balanced to the penny. When I close, it's always clean. When Carmen closes, it's not. The contrast keeps me up at night.
Missed the meeting again — impossible to leave on Valentine's Day. Three years in March and I still need those meetings. Maybe especially now.

Alejandro is threatening a lawyer now. Again. His latest email — CC'd to some attorney in Phoenix — demands Mom's medical records and financial accounting within 30 days. Sandra says to ignore the deadline. It has no legal force. But the stress is real.

I keep thinking about that bottle. Not a specific one — the idea of one. The theoretical drink that would make the email disappear for an hour. I know it doesn't work that way. I know the drink doesn't delete the problem — it multiplies it. But the fantasy persists.

Made it to Saturday meeting. Shared for the first time in weeks. Talked about feeling cornered from every direction — the brother, the work situation, the caregiving. People nodded. A woman named Janet said 'the walls aren't closing in — your awareness is expanding.' I'm going to sit with that for a while.
Dave says the 3-year mark is coming and I should be proud. I'll try.

Last night was bad. Couldn't sleep. Opened Instacart at 11 PM to order bar restock. Added Maker's Mark and Grey Goose to the cart. Told myself it was for the restaurant. Clicked order before I could think about it. But the restaurant doesn't need 3 bottles of bourbon and 2 bottles of vodka on a Wednesday night. I know what I was doing.
I didn't drink. I didn't open them. They're at the restaurant now. But I was closer than I've been in a long time.
Called Dave at 7 AM. He didn't judge. He never does.

Three years today. March 14, 2023 — the last drink. A Wednesday night. Maker's Mark, neat, at 1 AM in the Bella Notte kitchen after everyone went home. I sat on a milk crate next to the walk-in and drank half a bottle. Didn't even taste it after the third pour. Woke up on the kitchen floor at 4 AM with the cleaning crew staring at me.
Nobody knows that story except Dave and Dr. Martinez. Not Tony. Not Mom. Not the owner who trusted me with his restaurant.
Three years. 1,096 days. I've poured approximately 15,000 drinks for other people in that time and I haven't had one. Some days that feels like a miracle. Most days it just feels like Tuesday.

Couldn't make it to the Wednesday meeting tonight — we were slammed and short-staffed. I hate missing on the anniversary. Dave texted. He knows what today is. He said: the meeting is wherever you are, Vic. The program lives in you now.
I'm going to go sit with Mom for a while. She won't know what today means, but I will.

Dave asked if I'm tired or struggling. Honest answer: both. But he asked the right question because he knows there's a difference. Tired I can sleep off. Struggling means the disease is active. Right now it's mostly tired — but tired feeds struggling if I'm not careful.

Alejandro is coming to Milwaukee this week. Sandra prepped me: be cordial, let him see Mom, don't hand over documents, don't discuss legal matters. If he gets aggressive, end the visit. Simple instructions. Nothing about this is simple.

The register situation is also reaching a breaking point. Eight incidents. $2,400. I can't ignore it much longer. Sandra wants me to plan the confrontation and figure out who watches Mom if Carmen goes. There is no one. That's the problem.
Missed another meeting — was on the phone with Sandra until 8 PM. I'm letting the world push my meetings off the calendar. That has to stop.

Late-night whiskey inventory. It was just me and the walk-in full of cases. Maker's Mark, Woodford Reserve, Buffalo Trace. I was counting bottles for the weekly order and I stopped. Just stood there holding a bottle of Maker's and remembered what it felt like — the warmth, the way it killed every worried thought for 20 minutes.
I put the bottle down. I finished the count. I locked up and went to the car and sat there for ten minutes before I could drive home.
Texted Dave at 11 PM. He asked if I was tired or struggling. I said just tired. That wasn't completely honest. But by the time I got home and checked on Mom, the moment had passed.
I should have said struggling. Next time I'll say it. That's the commitment.

Missed Wednesday meeting again — Alejandro is coming to town and I was dealing with Sandra on the phone until 8 PM. I need to stop letting the world push my meetings off the calendar. That's how it starts.

Good day. Rosa arrived at 8 AM, gave vitamin D with breakfast (oatmeal and fruit). Mom was cheerful all day. They did a crossword puzzle together and watched her telenovela. She asked about the restaurant — 'Are they treating you well, mijo?' Clear and present.
All meals eaten. Donepezil at 8 PM with dinner. No issues.

Lucid all day. She asked about Alejandro — 'When is your brother coming to visit? He hasn't called in weeks.' She misses him. I don't know how to reconcile that with what he's doing. She loves him. He loves her. We just disagree violently about what that love should look like.
All three meals eaten. Meds taken. No episodes. Good day.

Episode at 7 PM. Called me Eduardo (Dad) when I got home from work. Took about 15 minutes to reorient. She kept saying 'Eduardo, you're late. Dinner is cold.' I played along until she settled. Rosa had already left for the day.
Skipped dinner — said she wasn't hungry. I made her chamomile tea and she drank that. Donepezil given with the tea. Second episode this month (first was Oct 8, mild).

Beautiful fall day. Rosa took her for a walk around the block. She named every neighbor's house and the flowers in their gardens. Memory for old things is still strong — it's the new things that slip away.
All meals, all meds. Good day.
Asked where Eduardo was again around 6 PM. Got upset when I tried to gently redirect. 'Don't lie to me, Victor. Where is your father?' She was agitated for about 30 minutes. Rosa had left at 4 PM.
Skipped lunch (Rosa says she refused). Ate breakfast and a small dinner after the episode passed. Third episode this month — within the expected 2-3/month range per Dr. Pham.

Calm day. Rosa reports she was pleasant and cooperative. They watched a cooking show together and Mom gave running commentary on the chef's technique. 'That's too much salt. He doesn't know what he's doing.' Sharp and funny.
All meals, all meds. No issues.

Alejandro called Mom this morning. Rosa says Mom was fine before the call and agitated after. She kept repeating 'Alejandro says I need to go somewhere. Where does he want me to go?' She refused lunch. Rosa tried to redirect but the anxiety persisted all afternoon.
I don't know exactly what Alejandro said. Need to think about whether limiting his phone contact is necessary. It's a hard line to draw — she loves hearing from him, but his agenda upsets her.

Made pozole with Rosa. She remembered the entire recipe without prompting — the chiles, the hominy, the pork. Rosa said she was in charge the whole time, giving orders like a head chef. 'More cumin, Rosa. You need more cumin.'
Made enough for the whole week. Days like this are a gift. All meals, all meds, no episodes.

Thanksgiving. Alejandro visited. No Rosa today (holiday). Mom was overwhelmed by the activity. She had at least two clear episodes — first around noon when she didn't recognize Alejandro at all ('Who is this man in my house?'), and again at 3 PM when she became agitated and insisted she needed to go home despite being home.

Alejandro was visibly shaken. I could see him building his case in real time. He pulled me aside in the kitchen: 'This is worse than I thought, Victor. She needs professional care.' Heated argument. Cousin Maria tried to mediate. Bad day.
Only ate breakfast. Refused everything else. Donepezil given but she was confused about it — 'What is this pill? I don't take pills.' Managed to get her to take it with applesauce.
Still rattled from Thanksgiving. Alejandro left yesterday but she kept asking 'When is the man leaving?' She doesn't remember his visit clearly but remembers being upset.

Confused most of the day. Ate breakfast and some lunch. Refused dinner. I sat with her all evening — it's my day off. She settled around 8 PM and we watched a movie together. She fell asleep on my shoulder. Peaceful by the end.

Good day. Rosa and Mom decorated the living room for Christmas. Mom was animated and happy — she directed where every ornament should go. 'The angel goes on top, Rosa. Always the angel on top.' She remembered the ornaments from years past.
All meals eaten, all meds taken. Four episodes in November — slightly up from October's three. Watching the trend.

Episode around 7 PM. Time confusion — she thought it was 7 AM and started getting ready for church. I tried to explain it was evening. She insisted it was morning. Wouldn't go to bed until almost 10 PM when exhaustion finally won.

Rosa says she was fine during the day. The episodes seem to cluster in the evenings. Dr. Pham calls it 'sundowning' — common with Alzheimer's. We discussed adjusting the Donepezil timing at the next appointment.

Carmen watched Mom tonight while I closed the restaurant. Rosa reported a good day. Carmen says Mom was pleasant all evening — they played cards and Mom won twice. Carmen gave Donepezil at 8 PM with no fuss.
All meals eaten. No episodes. Carmen is genuinely good with her. That's what makes the register situation so agonizing.
Christmas morning was beautiful. Mom opened gifts and smiled. She recognized the rosary I gave her — held it up and said 'This is like the one Mamá had.' Close enough. She was happy and present.

By evening the confusion came. She kept asking for Eduardo and asking when we were going to Midnight Mass. (We didn't go this year — too risky at night.) Episode around 8 PM. Settled by 9:30 with tea and chamomile.
Christmas is hard. The mix of lucid joy and evening confusion is the cruelest part of this disease. All meals eaten, meds given.
NYE. Carmen watched Mom while I ran the biggest service of the year. Mom was scared by the fireworks at midnight — thought there was a storm. Carmen calmed her down but she was up until almost 2 AM.
Skipped lunch. Ate breakfast and a small dinner. Five episodes in December — up from four in November. The progression is slow but measurable.
Good start to the new year. Mom was lucid all day. She asked me about my New Year's resolutions. I said 'Keep doing what I'm doing.' She said 'That's not ambitious enough, mijo.' She's right.
All meals, all meds, no episodes. January starting quiet.
Called the restaurant during lunch rush. I picked up in the office. 'Victor, tell your father to come home. He promised to fix the kitchen sink.' Dad has been dead for 11 years.
I told her Dad would be home soon. Staff doesn't know why she calls — I just say it's a family thing and step into the office. Rosa had already left. Episode lasted about 20 minutes on the phone.
Skipped dinner. Ate a snack around 7 PM. Meds given. Episodes are starting to happen more frequently — two already in January.

Bad day. Refused all meals except a few bites of toast at breakfast. Kept trying to leave the house — told Rosa she needed to go to the school to pick up 'the boys.' Rosa had to physically redirect her twice.
By the time I got home at 10:30 PM she was asleep, but Rosa's notes were alarming. This is the worst day-time episode we've had. Rosa handled it well but she looked shaken when I read her note.
Called Dr. Pham's office to discuss. Appointment moved up to next week.

Made tamales with Rosa. Sharp as a tack all day — directed the whole operation, remembered the recipe without any help. Rosa said she was giving instructions like a cooking school: 'The masa has to be THIS thin, Rosa.'
Made enough tamales for the week. I brought some to the restaurant. Tony said they're better than anything on our menu. Days like this make the bad days bearable. All meals, all meds.

Thought she needed to pick up the boys from school again. Same pattern as Jan 20 but less agitated — Rosa redirected more easily this time. Still, it's the fourth episode this month. Up from three in October, four in November, five in December.
The progression is clear. Dr. Pham says this is expected with early-stage Alzheimer's. Doesn't make it easier.

Alejandro called again. Same result — Mom was fine before, agitated after. She kept saying 'He says I have to leave. I don't want to leave my house, Victor.' Whatever he's telling her, it's hurting her.
Need to talk to Sandra about this. If his calls are causing documented distress episodes, is there a legal basis to limit contact? I don't want to go there but I can't keep watching this.
Good day. Watched telenovelas all afternoon. Rosa says she was singing along to the theme songs. These calm days are fewer but they still come. Hold on to them.
All meals, all meds. No episodes.

2 AM wandering. Found her in the kitchen trying to make coffee. Disoriented — didn't know where the cups were despite living here for 20 years. Talked her back to bed. She asked 'Why am I here?' and I said 'Because this is home, Mom.' She said 'It doesn't feel like home anymore.'
That one broke me. Didn't sleep after. Ate breakfast and lunch but the sleep deprivation is compounding.
Second night this week with wandering — found her in the hallway at 3 AM. She was calm this time but confused. Sleep is getting worse for both of us. Rosa says she's restless during nap time too.
Four episodes in February so far, and the month isn't over. The frequency is accelerating. Discussing with Dr. Pham at next appointment.

Bad day. Multiple episodes. Called Eduardo three times — once to Rosa, once to me at the restaurant, once to nobody (she was talking to an empty chair). Refused lunch and dinner. Only ate a few bites of breakfast.

Five episodes this month. The trend line is unmistakable. October: 3. November: 4. December: 5. January: 4. February: 5. But the severity is increasing — the wandering, the refusal to eat, the duration of confusion.
Beautiful day. Mom helped Rosa make tamales again. She remembered the entire recipe — the masa, the filling, the assembly. Sharp, funny, bossy in the kitchen.

Tony said the tamales were better than anything on our menu. Days like this make me wonder if the diagnosis is wrong. I know it's not. Dr. Pham showed me the scans. But when she's like this, it's hard to believe what's coming.
All meals, all meds, no episodes. More days like this, please.
Alejandro called Mom this morning. Rosa said Mom was fine before the call and agitated after. She kept saying 'Alejandro says I have to leave. I don't want to leave my house.'

Refused lunch. Sat in her chair barely speaking to Rosa all afternoon. I need to talk to Sandra about limiting his phone contact. I know it's a nuclear option. But I can't have him winding her up from 1,700 miles away.
Small dinner (soup and bread). Donepezil given. Settled for bed around 9:30.

She remembered my birthday today. My birthday is in August, not March, but she remembered it was important — she said 'Happy birthday, mijo' and gave me a hug. It's not my birthday. But she remembered that I exist and that I matter. That's enough.

This is my sobriety anniversary. Three years today. I sat with her after Rosa left and we looked at old photo albums. She pointed at Dad in every picture and named him correctly. She pointed at Alejandro and called him Victor. She pointed at me and called me Eduardo. But she was smiling and present and happy.
All meals eaten. All meds taken. No episode. A gift of a day.

Called the restaurant at 6:15 PM during dinner rush asking for Dad. 'Tell Eduardo I need him to come home. He promised he'd fix the sink.' I stepped into the office and talked her through it for 8 minutes. Told her Dad was running an errand.

Only ate breakfast and lunch. Refused dinner. Donepezil given with chamomile tea. Up at 2 AM confused — thought she was in Guadalajara at her parents' house. Back to sleep by 2:45. I did not sleep after that.

I was out most of the day for the Alvarez quinceañera catering. Rosa stayed late (until 6 PM) and Carmen came over at 5:30 to overlap. Mom ate well. Rosa reported she was cheerful — they watched a movie together.
Carmen gave Donepezil at 8 PM. Mom was asleep by 9:30. Carmen stayed until I got home at 11 PM.
I hate depending on Carmen for this. Every time she watches Mom, I think about the register. And then I think about what happens to Mom's evenings if I let Carmen go. There's no clean answer here.

Episode at 2 PM. Rosa called me at the restaurant — Mom became agitated and insisted she needed to pick up 'the boys' from school. She was talking about me and Alejandro as children. Rosa redirected but it took 30 minutes.
Skipped lunch. Rosa made a snack at 3:30 PM (crackers and cheese). By evening she was settled but foggy. Donepezil given with soup.
Up at 2 AM confused. Thought she was at her parents' house. Talked her back. No sleep for me after that. Six episodes in March already and we're not done. This is 3 per week now.

Good day. Rosa arrived 8 AM, gave vitamin D with breakfast (oatmeal and fruit). Mom ate well — all three meals. Lucid all day. Watched her telenovela and worked on a word search puzzle. Asked about the garden — wants to plant tomatoes when it warms up.
Donepezil at 8 PM with dinner. No resistance. She asked 'How's the restaurant, mijo?' Clear as a bell.
No episode. Good sleep last night. The good days are getting rarer but they still come.
⚠️ CONFIDENTIAL — Do not share with any staff, including Carmen. Do not share with Giancarlo until legal counsel advises.
Summary
Pattern of register shortages at Bella Notte, all occurring on shifts where Carmen Diaz is the closing lead. 8 incidents over 3 months (January – March 2026), totaling $2,400.

Background: On December 18, 2025, I first noticed something felt off with the register after Carmen's close. The cash drawer seemed light, but I hadn't been formally tracking at that point and didn't document the exact amount. That nagging feeling is what made me start paying attention. Formal tracking began in January.
Incident Log (Canonical — from Airtable)

rows: Thursday close. First formally documented discrepancy. Light night, ~130 covers.
Sunday close. Second incident. Started pulling old reports.
Friday close. Third in January. Three in one month is not coincidence.
Saturday close. Biggest yet. Busy night, ~180 covers. Higher cash volume.
Thursday close. Smallest amount. Lighter night. Takes less when there's less cash?
Saturday close. Largest single discrepancy. Running total hits $2,400.

blocks.text: Pattern Analysis
All 8 incidents occurred on Carmen's closing shifts.
Average shortage: $300 per incident.
No shortages on nights when Victor closes the register himself (checked 14 nights in period).
No shortages on the 4 nights Daniela covered closing.
Carmen closed every one of these nights. Other leads (Daniela, me) — all reconciled perfectly.
Shortages occur on both slow and busy nights — but amounts tend to be larger on busier nights with higher cash volume.
Amounts range from $150 to $430. Consistent enough to suggest a deliberate pattern, variable enough to suggest she's reading the night's cash volume.
Cross-Reference with Shift Schedule

Pulled shift schedule from Airtable. Carmen closed 24 nights in the 3-month period. 8 of those (33%) had shortages. The other 16 nights balanced within normal tolerance (±$20). She's not stealing every shift — she's selective.
Complication

Carmen is the only person besides me who watches Mom on close nights. If I fire Carmen, I have no evening caregiver for the 3–4 nights per week I close the restaurant. Rosa only works days (8 AM – 4 PM). I can't afford a night aide — not with the Comfort Keepers overcharge eating $600/month I don't have.
Complication: Carmen watches Mom on specific dates when I close the restaurant. She is genuinely good with Mom — patient, kind, attentive. Mom likes her. If I fire her, who takes care of Mom?
Sandra says document everything before confronting. I'm documenting.
Next Steps
Consult Sandra Milovich on legal exposure — what are my obligations as GM?
Discuss with Sandra: if I fire Carmen, does Giancarlo need to know? What's my liability if I DON'T tell him?
Research evening caregiving options (agency, private aide, neighbor) as contingency
Continue logging — do NOT confront Carmen until plan is in place
Timeline of Alejandro's escalation and my responses. Keep updated for Sandra Milovich.
Background

Alejandro (brother, Phoenix AZ) wants Mom moved to a memory care facility. He does not contribute financially to her current home care. He does not have power of attorney. Mom lives with me and I am her primary caregiver. Sandra Milovich (elder law attorney) is advising me.
Timeline
October 5, 2025 — First Concern

Alejandro called, concerned about Mom's forgetfulness. Said she 'sounded confused' on the phone last week and he thinks she needs 'professional care.' I told him she HAS professional care — a daily aide, a neurologist, and me. He said 'you're not a professional, Victor.' Heated call. Ended badly.
November 27, 2025 — Thanksgiving Visit Fallout

Alejandro visited for Thanksgiving (arrived Nov 26). Saw Mom confused — she didn't recognize him at one point. He was shaken. Pushed hard for facility research. Sent me brochures for three memory care facilities in Milwaukee within a week of leaving.
December 10, 2025 — First POA Mention in Family Group Chat

First mention of POA in family group chat. Alejandro said: 'If you won't cooperate on getting Mom proper care, I'll file for power of attorney.' Maria (cousin, Chicago) tried to mediate. I didn't respond in the group chat — too angry.
December 8, 2025 — Facility Brochures

Alejandro sent facility brochures via email with a note: 'I've researched these. The Sunrise on Bluemound has memory care units starting at $6,500/month. We need to discuss this seriously.' I responded: absolutely not. Mom stays home. She's my responsibility.
December — January: Quiet Period
Alejandro went relatively quiet through the holidays. Maria said he was 'regrouping.' I focused on the restaurant's holiday season and the register situation.
January 5, 2026 — Escalation
Alejandro texted: 'If you won't consider options, I'll have to take action.' I didn't respond. Forwarded to Sandra for the file.
February 8, 2026 — Attorney Threat
Alejandro emailed: 'My attorney will contact you. I've retained Douglas Hartfield in Phoenix to explore our legal options regarding Mom's care.' Formal tone. CC'd the attorney.
February 20, 2026 — Sandra Retained for POA Defense

Retained Sandra Milovich formally for POA defense (she was already advising on Comfort Keepers billing). Sandra's assessment: Alejandro's legal position is weak. He has no POA, no court order, and no evidence of neglect. In Wisconsin, POA requires principal's consent or court-appointed guardianship. Sandra recommends I proactively seek a limited guardianship appointment to formalize my authority.
March 5, 2026 — Formal Records Demand

Alejandro demands Mom's medical and financial records formally, CC'd to his attorney. Threatening legal action if not provided within 30 days. Sandra responded on my behalf declining the request and citing lack of legal authority. Sandra's advice: ignore the deadline, it has no legal force.
March 20, 2026 — Visit Announced

Alejandro announced he's coming to Milwaukee 'next week to assess the situation.' Maria offered to come down from Chicago to mediate. Sandra and I discussed visit preparation: be cordial, let him see Mom, do NOT hand over documents, do NOT discuss legal matters, end the visit if he becomes aggressive.
Sandra's Current Assessment (March 2026)

Alejandro's legal position is weak. No POA, no court order, no evidence of neglect. Guardianship petition being prepared on my behalf. Biggest risk: if Alejandro finds something to allege neglect — care log, Dr. Pham records, and medication compliance must be impeccable.

Secondary risk: if Alejandro discovers my financial strain (Comfort Keepers overcharge, register theft losses, credit card debt), he could argue I'm financially unfit to manage Mom's affairs. Personal financial information must stay private.
